title: Striga Witchweed
symptoms: Also known as purple witchweed, this plant is characterized by green stems and leaves as well as small, bright purple flowers. The weed parasitizes crops and sucks water and nutrients from the host plant, leading to symptoms similar to drought or nutrient deficiency: yellowing, wilting leaves, and stunted growth. Symptoms before the weed appears are difficult to diagnose accurately because they resemble common nutrient deficiencies. Once witchweed appears, it is too late to mitigate the damage, even by removing the weed. This can result in significant yield losses.
organic_control: Witchweed is one of the most difficult parasitic plants to control, mainly due to the high number of seeds it produces and its lifespan. Affected plants should be uprooted and burned before the weed flowers. The fungus Fusarium oxysporum can be used as a biological control method against witchweed because the fungus infects the new vascular tissues of the Striga plant and impairs its growth.
chemical_control: Always consider an integrated approach combining preventative measures with biological treatments if available. Available witchweed herbicides are quite expensive and can also directly affect crops. Sprayers need to be protected and herbicides can kill beneficial plants. Seed herbicides have been successfully applied to millet and sorghum with weed infestation rates reduced by up to 80%. The method involves soaking herbicide-resistant seeds in a herbicide solution before planting.
cause: Symptoms caused by The parasitic plant Striga hermonthica, commonly known as purple witch weed or giant witch weed, is a particularly serious problem in Sub-Saharan Africa. It can be a dangerous problem for cereal crops, rice, and legumes including rice, corn, millet, sorghum, sugarcane, and string beans. Each witch weed plant can produce between 90,000 and 500,000 seeds, which can survive in the soil for over 10 years. These seeds overwinter in the soil after being dispersed by wind, water, animals, or human machinery. When favorable weather conditions arise, and when they are a few centimeters from the base of the host plant, they begin to germinate. Upon contact with the roots, the witch weed creates a structure that attaches it to the host plant, thereby establishing a parasitic relationship. Nitrogen-rich fertilizers also reduce witch weed infestation rates.
prevention: Using plant growth regulators to enhance plant health. Apply plenty of manure early in the season. Sow Semolina plants in the fields to prevent witches' leaf spot from attaching to crop roots. Planting elephant grass around the fields also helps pull witches' leaf spot away from main crops. Clean all tools, equipment, clothing, and shoes carefully after working with infected plants. Apply appropriate nitrogen fertilizer. Plan your crop rotation with legumes.
summary: A plant with bright green leaves and small, vibrant purple flowers that grows around the field. Symptoms of the host plant are stunting, wilting, and fading.

title: Fruit Cracking Disease in Tomatoes
symptoms: Symptoms appear as cracks on the outer skin of the fruit. The cracks vary in depth and size, usually appearing around the top of the fruit. The concentric or radial symmetry of the lesions may indicate different physiological problems the plant is experiencing. Sometimes the bottom of the fruit is also affected. The younger the fruit, the greater the damage caused by the cracks. Cracks may appear around the stem. This disorder is caused by the fruit growing excessively beyond the capacity of the fruit's skin. The elasticity of the fruit's skin is subjected to excessive tension, causing small cracks to appear and gradually break.Cracking out.
organic_control: There are no biological controls for this disease. It can only be treated with preventative measures.
chemical_control: Always consider the possibility of combining preventative and biological treatments, to the extent applicable. This disease can only be treated with preventative measures. At the same time, avoid over-fertilizing with nitrogen and pay attention to the potassium content in the soil.
cause: Cracking occurs due to the fruit developing too quickly and suddenly, often associated with the plant absorbing excess water. Such sudden changes in plant development can be triggered or exacerbated by changes in environmental conditions such as wet and cold weather, high humidity, following periods of hot and dry weather. A balanced fertilization regime is also important to help the plant avoid this situation. For example: over-fertilizing with nitrogen and under-fertilizing with potassium during the flowering and fruiting stages.This is a factor that causes excessive fruit growth, leading to the appearance of cracks.
prevention: Choose tomato varieties with high resistance to cracking. Avoid overwatering; aim for a stable water supply to the garden instead of sudden flooding. Pay attention to upcoming extreme weather events and conditions of high humidity and low sunlight. Maintain a stable balance between the number of leaves and fruit on each plant to avoid stressing the plant. At the same time, avoid over-fertilizing with nitrogen and under-fertilizing with potassium.
summary: Excessive stress on the fruit skin causes cracks to appear. Cracks can also appear around the stem.

title: Tomato Catface Disease
symptoms: Tomato Catface is a physiological disorder that causes the fruit to develop abnormally with the formation of scars, usually appearing at the end of the flowering stage. Affected fruits often have a lobed shape with brown scars appearing between the lobes and potentially penetrating deep into the flesh. This symptom should not be confused with concentric or radial fruit cracking in tomato fruit cracking disease. Although the commercial value is lost, such deformed fruits retain their normal flavor and are completely safe to use. Possible causes of this symptom include... The above is due to cold weather, with nighttime temperatures below 12°C, occurring during the flowering stage, high nitrogen content, and herbicide damage. Tomato varieties that produce large fruits are more susceptible to this disease than others.
organic_control: This disease can only be controlled by preventative measures.
chemical_control: Always consider the possibility of combining preventative measures and biological treatments, where applicable. This disease can only be controlled by easily implemented preventative measures. On the other hand, avoid using herbicides that can trigger conditions leading to this disease in susceptible tomato varieties.
cause: The exact cause of tomato fruit malformation is not yet determined, but generally the symptoms mentioned above appear more frequently in tomato varieties that produce large fruits. There is a correlation between prolonged low nighttime temperatures (below 12°C) and other factors. For several consecutive days during the bud development stage, this physiological disorder may be due to incomplete pollination. Some tomato varieties are more sensitive to such prolonged temperature changes than others. Conditions that hinder bud development also lead to deformed fruit. Mechanical damage to the flowers, excessive pruning, or exposure to certain herbicides (2,4-D) can also lead to deformed fruit. Finally, damage caused by thrips or a condition called tomato stunting syndrome can also lead to deformed fruit.
prevention: Use varieties with high tolerance to temperature changes. Avoid using herbicides as this can lead to this disease. Regularly monitor the temperature during the bud development stage. Avoid causing mechanical damage to the plants during garden work. Maintain a regular schedule.Monitor nitrogen levels in the soil before fertilizing.
summary: Fruits begin to develop deformities with severe scarring and cracks at the end of the flowering stage.

title: Tomato Scarring Disease
symptoms: Scarring is a physiological disorder resulting in the formation of thin, brown necrotic scars running along the length of the fruit, creating localized cracks. The scars develop partly along the length and width of the fruit, sometimes connecting the bottom of the fruit to the stem in a zipper-like lesion. Deep grooves in the scar tissue, deforming the fruit surface, are also common symptoms. Damaged tissues lose their elasticity and the fruit cannot develop properly. Once the damage has occurred, any treatment is too late.
organic_control: There are no biological controls for this disease. It can only be treated with preventative measures.
chemical_control: If possible, always consider a combination of preventative and biological treatments. This disease can only be treated with preventative measures.
cause: Scarring is a physiological disorder caused by low temperature and high humidity conditions at the end of the flowering and beginning of the fruiting stage. During the development of young fruit, one or more anthers remain attached to the ovary wall, creating small scars on the outside of the fruit as it grows. Temperature sensitivity varies depending on the tomato variety. Some tomato varieties are more sensitive than others to temperature changes, with the Beefsteak tomato variety being one of the least tolerant.
prevention: Plant varieties that are resistant to cold weather. Ensure optimal environmental conditions for the plants during the flowering and fruiting stages. Avoid exposing the plants to low temperatures, excessive dryness, or excessive humidity at night. Remove scarred fruit early to help the plant conserve energy and nutrients.
summary: Characteristic thin necrotic scars run along the stem of the fruit. The scars can spread throughout the entire fruit.The length of the fruit extends to the base. This disorder occurs due to low temperature and high humidity conditions during the fruiting stage of the plant.

title: Alternaria Leaf Blight
symptoms: Young plants are resistant to the pathogen. The lowermost leaves are always the first to show signs of infection, which gradually spreads to the upper leaves. Infection begins as small, oval, chlorotic lesions which are irregularly scattered on the lowermost leaves, gradually spreading to the upper leaves. Over time, the lesions enlarge, become irregular in shape and develop into dark brown or grey sunken lesions. Lesions may have a bright yellow marginal zone and can grow up to 1cm or more in diameter. Under moist conditions, lesions may be covered by black powdery conidia. At later stages of the disease, lesions will merge together resulting in the death of the entire leaf. In severe cases, leaf sheaths, awns and glumes are also affected and have a burnt appearance.
organic_control: Seedborne infections can be controlled by treating seed with vitavax @ 2.5 per kg seed. A mixture of Trichoderma viride and vitavax effectively hinder further infections (by 98.4%). Mix Urea @ 2 - 3% with Zineb at first and second sprays. Apply aqueous Neem Leaf extracts. Fungicide and hot water treatments have been used to reduce seedborne inoculum. Pathogens Trichoderma viride (2%) and T. harzianum(2%), Aspergillus humicola and Bacillus subtilis are often used to control further outbreak of the disease.
chemical_control: Always consider an integrated approach with preventive measures together with biological treatments, if available. A. triticina can be controlled through the application of fungicides with a 75% reduction in disease severity and an increase in plant yield. Apply fungicides such as mancozeb, ziram, zineb (0.2%), thiram, fytolan, propineb, chlorothalonil and nabam, propiconazole (0.15%), tebuconazole, and hexaconazole (0.5%). To prevent developing tolerance to mancozeb, apply a combination of fungicides.
cause: Damage is caused by the fungus Alternaria triticina. Infection is both soilborne and seedborne and can be dispersed by wind. Infected seeds may be smaller than healthy ones and are often shriveled, with brown discoloration. Plants are affected when planted into infected soils or getting in contact with infested crop residues (e.g., by rain splashes or by direct contact). The fungus survives reportedly two months in infected debris on the soil surface during summer, but four months in buried debris. Susceptibility increases with plant age as A. triticina is unable to infect young wheat seedlings which are under about four weeks of age. The symptoms are usually not evident until the plants are about seven weeks of age. Temperatures of 20 - 25°C are optimal for infection and disease development. Under severe conditions, yield losses may exceed 80%.
prevention: Use less-susceptible varieties as well as healthy seeds from certified sources. Practice the best hygiene measures. Infected crops should be destroyed by burning and ploughing. This will prevent aerial dispersal of the pathogen via infected crop residues. Herbicides can be used to destroy the infected crops. Infested fields should not be resown for a minimum of two years. Disposable equipment, infected plant material or soil should be disposed of by autoclaving, high-temperature incineration or deep burial. Any equipment removed from the site for disposal should be double-bagged. Deeply incorporate crop debris after harvest to reduce pathogen survival and primary inoculum that could incite future epidemics. Promote air movement within the canopy by planting rows parallel to prevailing wind direction, reducing plant population and planting on wider row spacing. Avoid irrigating near dusk and following overhead irrigation, if possible to reduce periods of leaf wetness. Apply adequate fertilizers.
summary: The disease appears when wheat plants are 7-8 weeks old. The condition becomes severe when the crop is mature. Small, oval, discolored lesions can be found scattered on the leaves in an irregular manner. As the lesions enlarge, they become dark brown to grey and irregular in shape.

title: Rose Mildew
symptoms: This disease is characterized by the dense, persistent and grayish patches of fungal growth on leaves, stems, inflorescences and fruits. Over time, the powdery coating causes the distortion and discoloration of leaves and premature leaf fall in severe cases. Buds often fail to unfold normally or be destroyed. Twigs may show a stunted growth. Fruits develop discoloration and lesions that prevents further normal development. In case of severe infections, the skin eventually turns dark brown, with the surface becoming leathery and hard, often subjected to cracks. Hosts include apricot, sour cherries and peach.
organic_control: Products based on the antagonist fungus Ampelomyces quisqualis and the bacteria Bacillus subtilis have been used as biological agents to control Podosphaera pannosa. Several organic fungicides are also available in the form of horticultural oils, neem oil, jojoba oil, potassium bicarbonate and bicarbonate of soda.
chemical_control: Always consider an integrated approach with preventive measures and biological treatments if available. Formulations based on thiophanate-methyl, propiconazole and sulfur fungicides have been recommended for powdery mildews in general, including powdery mildew of rose. Other products that have been used with some success include dinitrophenols and  compounds based on pyridine and pyrimidine.
cause: The disease is caused on roses and stone fruits by the fungus Podosphaera pannosa, which survives the winter in a circular, spore-producing structure. Under favorable conditions in spring, this structure breaks open and releases spores that are later spread by wind, insects, and rain. When they land on a susceptible host and germinates, they start producing the characteristic “powdery” appearance on leaves or fruits. Optimal conditions for powdery mildew of rose are 16-27 °C, with the optimal temperature for fungal growth at 23 °C. Shaded areas are also preferred since the prolonged exposure to light reduces its growth. Moreover, these fungi do not need moisture to germinate and infect their hosts. In fact, prolonged leaf moisture inhibits rather than promotes fungal growth. The use of silicon as part of a fertilization program can also reduce the occurrence of powdery mildew.
prevention: The selection of resistant varieties is crucial to avoid fungal growth and development of symptoms. Monitor the orchards for signs of the disease. Choose location well exposed to the sun and with good aeration. Complement the fertilization program with silicon. Avoid irrigating in late afternoon or evening. Keep weeds and grasses under control under trees with low hanging branches. Prune trees in a way that allow a good ventilation of the canopy.
summary: Dense, persistent and grayish patches of fungal growth on leaves, stems, inflorescences and fruits. Powdery coating causes the formation of lesions, distortion and discoloration of leaves and fruits. In case of severe infections, the fruit skin eventually turns dark brown, and cracks may appear.

title: Apple Scabies
symptoms: The first visible symptoms of apple scab appear in spring as small, circular, olive-green spots on the leaves, typically along the main veins. As these spots spread, they turn brown-black and eventually merge into large necrotic areas. Infected leaves often become distorted and fall prematurely, leading to a sparse canopy in severe cases. On shoots, the affected areas swell and crack, providing entry points for further infection. On fruits, brown to black, circular lesions develop on the surface. These lesions enlarge over time, become raised and rough, restricting fruit expansion and causing misshapen fruits with cracked skin exposing the inner flesh. Light exposure does not significantly affect fruit quality. Additionally, these scab spots can serve as entry points for secondary pathogens and rot development within the fruit flesh, reducing fruit quality and storage life.
organic_control: If this disease has been severe in previous seasons, applying copper-based fungicides in winter can help prevent fungal development on the tree. Sulfur applications may provide limited control against apple scab. However, sulfur-containing and pyrethrin-based solutions are viable organic options for managing this disease during the growing season.
chemical_control: If there is a possibility, always consider using an integrated approach combining preventive measures with biological treatments. Spraying preventive fungicides such as dodine, captan or dinathion around the bud and shoot wounds can help prevent this disease. When the scab disease is detected, it is possible to use fungicides containing propiconazole, myclobutanil or sulfur to control the development of this fungus. It is recommended to use different classes of fungicides when treating this disease to avoid the development of fungicide resistance.
cause: Apple scab is caused by the fungus Venturia inaequalis. This fungus survives the winter mainly on fallen infected leaves and also on bud scales and wounds on the bark of trees. In early spring, the fungus resumes growth and begins producing spores, which are then dispersed by wind. When these spores land on developing leaves and fruits, they initiate a new infection process. The outer parts of young fruit and shoots are particularly susceptible to this disease. However, mature fruits become less susceptible. Wet conditions and prolonged leaf and fruit wetness are essential factors for the development of this disease. Other host plants for this fungus include various shrubs belonging to the genus Cotoneaster, Pyracantha (firethorn) and Sorbus (rowan). All apple varieties are susceptible to this scab disease, with Gala being particularly vulnerable.
prevention: Plant resistant varieties. Monitor the orchard for signs of the disease. Remove and destroy infected leaves, shoots and fruits. Clean up fallen leaves around the tree after harvest. Another method is to spray a 5% urea solution on fallen leaves in autumn to accelerate their decomposition and hinder the life cycle of the fungus. You can also pile up leaves to speed up their decomposition process. Ensure proper pruning to create good air circulation within the tree canopy. Water plants in the late afternoon or early morning and avoid watering on the foliage. Avoid creating a humid environment under the canopy when watering plants. Apply lime after leaf fall to increase soil pH. Spread a layer of mulch under the tree, keeping it away from the trunk.

title: Rust Disease Affecting Beans
symptoms: The first symptoms are small, pale yellow spots on the upper surface of older leaves, typically appearing on the underside. As the disease progresses, these spots develop into raised, brownish-black pustules that rupture and release spores. The affected leaves may yellow, wilt, and fall prematurely. The disease can also affect stems and pods, leading to reduced yield and quality.
organic_control: Biological control agents such as Bacillus subtilis, Arthrobacter, and Streptomyces species have shown significant effectiveness in managing this disease.
chemical_control: In all cases, consider the possibility of applying biological prevention and treatment measures to the extent possible. Pesticides such as triazole and strobilurin are significantly effective in controlling this disease.
cause: This disease originates from a fungus with the scientific name Uromyces appendiculatus. Overwintering in plant debris left on the soil, this fungus is an obligate parasite, requiring plant tissue to survive. Initially, fungal infection occurs when fungal spores are dispersed onto plants by wind, water, and insects. This fungus thrives in conditions of high humidity and temperature. Fungal spores can proliferate very quickly under such conditions. This disease often causes serious damage during periods of hot and humid weather.
prevention: Choose to plant varieties with high resistance or tolerance. Do not plant legumes on these The land has previously been infected with this disease. Clear weeds and naturally growing plants from the bean field. Avoid overwatering or using overhead sprinkler irrigation systems. Cut and remove all infected plant parts. After harvesting, clean up and dispose of all plant debris. Choose a planting date and plan care and watering carefully to avoid prolonged leaf dampness in high temperatures. Avoid excessive nitrogen fertilization and ensure adequate potassium fertilization.
Summary: Tiny yellowish-brown nodules surrounded by a yellow halo appear on the underside of older leaves. Similar symptoms may also appear on leaf petioles, stems, and fruits. Leaves turn yellow and wilt.

title: Brown rust disease in barley
symptoms: The first symptoms appear in late winter to spring when small, round, orange-brown nodules are scattered randomly on the upper surface of the leaves. These contain spores that will increase the infection process between barley plants. Occasionally, these nodules also develop on the stems, leaf sheaths, and panicles. They are often surrounded by a yellow or green halo. Later in the season (late spring or early summer), small black nodules gradually develop on the underside of the leaves. These new sacs contain spores that will later survive on root shoots or alternative host plants to restart the cycle. Unlike the light brown nodules, the black nodules do not peel off to the fingers when rubbed.
organic_control: To date, there is no biological control solution for barley brown rust. Please contact us if you know of any.
chemical_control: If possible, always consider using a combination of methods. Preventive measures include biological treatments. Timely spraying of prothioconazole-based fungicides will generally help control rust disease. There are also many foliar fungicides available to treat rust disease in barley. For best prevention, spray them when rust disease is first detected. Additional sprays may be necessary when the season is favorable for rust disease.
cause: Symptoms caused by the fungus Puccinia hordei, one of four fungi that cause rust disease in barley. These organisms can only grow on green plants. In the case of P. hordei, it survives the summer on late shoots and alternative host plants such as grass lilies (Ornithogalum umbellatum). Warm temperatures (15° to 22°C) with high humidity and frequent rainfall create conditions for disease development, while dry, windy days support the spread of spores. Severe brown rust disease attacks barley host plants.Weakness occurs late in the season, especially if high doses of nitrogen have been applied. Early-sown crops are more severely infected than late-sown crops, especially when the weather is warm. However, brown rust in barley is rarely a problem if the crop is treated with fungicides.
prevention: Plant disease-resistant varieties, as this is the most effective way to break the pathogen's cycle. Avoid early planting. Monitor the field for symptoms starting in early spring. Remove weeds and alternative host plants after harvest to prevent disease transmission.
summary: Small, orange-brown nodules on the upper surface of the leaves. Nodules with a halo around them. Black nodules on the underside of the leaves.

title: Late blight disease in potatoes
symptoms: Dark brown spots develop on the leaves starting from the tip or edges. In humid climates, these spots become baked-out lesions. A white fungal coating may be visible on the underside of the leaves. As the disease progresses, the entire leaf necrotizes, turns brown, and dies. Similar lesions develop on the stem and petioles. Potato tubers have grayish-green spots on the skin, and the flesh also turns brown, rendering them inedible. The rotting condition of infected fields emits a characteristic odor.
organic_control: Use copper-based fungicides before dry weather. Spraying leaves with organic coatings can also prevent infection.
chemical_control: If possible, always consider a combination of preventative and biological treatments. The use of fungicides is crucial in controlling late blight, especially in humid areas. Fungicides that come into contact with the leaves are effective. Before infection and does not cause resistance in the fungus. Fungicides containing mandipropamid, chlorothalonil, fluazinam, or mancozeb can also be used as a preventative treatment. Treating seeds before sowing with fungicides such as mancozeb is also effective.
cause: This fungus is an obligate parasite. This means it must survive the winter in leaf and tuber debris as well as on alternate host plants to survive. It enters the plant through wounds and tears in the tuber skin. Fungal spores germinate at higher temperatures in the spring and spread via wind or water. The disease is worse on cool nights (below 18°C), warm days (18-22°C), and prolonged wet conditions such as rain and fog (90% relative humidity). Under these conditions, late blight outbreaks can occur.
prevention: Use healthy seeds or more disease-resistant plants. Ensure good ventilation and well-drained soil in the field. Monitor the field and remove infected plants and surrounding plants. Apply crop rotation for two to three years with non-host plants. Destroy stray host plants growing in and around the field. Avoid excessive nitrogen fertilization. Use plant growth regulators. Store tubers at low temperatures and in good ventilation. Dispose of tubers and leaf residues after harvest by burying them 0.5 meters deep or feeding them to animals.
summary: Dark brown spots on leaf tips and edges. Spots turn into transparent lesions. White fungus covers the underside of leaves. Leaves wilt and die. Grayish-green spots on potato tubers.

title: Black mold disease on tomatoes
symptoms: This pathogenic fungus causes two main groups of symptoms in pomegranate and tomato plants, but these do not necessarily occur simultaneously. Commonly referred to as black spot and fruit rot, the symptoms vary depending on the variety. Black spot is characterized by the appearance of small, round, reddish-brown or black spots (approximately 1-3 mm in size), surrounded by a yellowish-green halo, on the fruit and leaves. As the disease progresses, these spots merge to form larger patches, potentially covering up to 50% of the fruit's surface. On leaves, the spots turn yellow and may cause premature leaf drop. The outer part of the fruit begins to rot while the edible tissues inside remain unaffected. Slightly unusual changes in peel color or fruit shape may be external signs of internal rot, but the fruit usually retains a healthy appearance until harvest. The rotting seed coat becomes visible only after the fruit is cut open.
organic_control: There are currently no biological control measures to combat the fungus Alternaria alternata. However, copper oxychloride-based products have been shown to be highly effective in controlling this disease in pomegranate trees as well as in tomato plants.
chemical_control: Always consider the possibility of combining preventative and biological control measures, where applicable. Applying preventative sprays twice during the flowering stage or when the first symptoms appear on the fruit can effectively control the disease. Products containing propiconazole, thiophanate methyl, or azoxystrobine have proven highly effective. It is important to adhere to regulations regarding pesticide concentrations and to use different fungicide formulations to avoid developing resistance.
cause: Black spot and fruit rot symptoms can originate from several species of fungi belonging to the Alternaria genus, but the primary causative agent is the fungus scientifically known as Alternaria alternata. These fungi often survive on plant debris, spoiled fruit, or on the ground. Their spores are carried by the wind to flower buds. Insects and birds also help spread the spores. Infection thrives in rainy and humid weather, especially during the late flowering or early fruit development stages. Typically, fruit rot is only detected after harvest, during storage, or during transport. This fungus commonly grows inside pomegranates and tomatoes, causing the fruit to rot and become unsaleable.
prevention: Use healthy planting materials from certified sources. Ensure efficient drainage in the garden, as waterlogged conditions stress the plants and can lead to fruit cracking. Inspect plants and the garden for any signs of disease, especially during the flowering stage. Fertilize plants appropriately to help them strengthen their natural resistance. Collect all infected fruit and destroy them by burning. While they may look healthy, infected fruit can still fall to the ground when the plant is gently shaken at harvest time. Remove old fruit and dead branches from the garden. Sort tomatoes, as well as pomegranates, carefully at harvest to avoid disease transmission during storage and transport.
summary: Small, reddish-brown, round spots appear on the leaves and fruit, surrounded by a yellowish-green halo. The spots gradually spread. The fruit rots.

title: Leaf mold disease of tomatoes
symptoms: Symptoms often appear on both sides of the foliage and sometimes appear on the fruit. Old leaves are affected first, then the disease gradually progresses and spreads to young leaves. On the upper surface of the leaves, small pale green or yellowish spots appear, with unclear borders. On the underside of the leaves, dark green, purple-gray, velvety patches appear just below the leaf spots. These are patches of fungal structures that produce spores, and the spore spots have been treated. over time, as the spots grow, the color of the infected leaves changes from yellowish (yellowing) to brown (dead tissue) and the leaves begin to dry and curl prematurely, leading to leaf drop as the disease progresses to a severe level. Sometimes, the fungus causes various symptoms in the flowers or fruit, which may turn black and will die before fruiting black, velvety color on the fruit stem. As the disease progresses, the infected area becomes sunken, dry and tough.
organic_control: Seeds should be treated with hot water (122°F or 50°C for 25 minutes) to combat seed pathogens. The fungi Acremonium strictum, Dicyma pulvinata, Trichoderma harzianum or T. viride and Trichothecium roseum are antagonistic to the mold M. fulva, and they can be used to reduce the likelihood of its spread. In these In a greenhouse test, the growth of M. fulva on tomatoes was inhibited by A. strictum, Trichoderma viride strain 3 and T. roseum by 53%, 66% and 84%, respectively. On a smaller scale, spraying with a mixture of apple cider vinegar, garlic and milk can be used to control this mold.
chemical_control: Always consider the possibility of combining preventative measures with biological treatments, to the extent applicable. The use of chemicals should be carried out before the plant becomes infected with the fungus when environmental conditions are favorable for the development of the disease. Recommended preparations for use in the field are chlorothalonil, maneb, mancozeb and copper-based chemical formulations. For greenhouses, it is recommended to use chemicals such as difenoconazole, mandipropamide, cymoxanil, famoxadone and cyprodinil
cause: The above symptoms come from a mold called Mycovellosiella fulva. The spores of this fungus can survive without a host for 6 months to a year at room temperature (living in such conditions is not required). Prolonged humidity and humidity above 85% are favorable conditions for spores to produce. Spores need temperature conditions from 4°C to 34°C to grow. For germination, the optimum temperature is 24-26°C. Dry conditions and lack of water on the outside of the leaves are detrimental to their germination. Symptoms usually begin to appear about 10 days after grafting, with the formation of spots on both sides of the leaf blade, large amounts of spore-producing fungal structures are formed and the spores are easily spread from one plant to another by wind and water, through tools and water. work equipment, garden workers' clothing and insects. Pathogens often infect leaves by penetrating through leaf pores (stomata) in high humidity conditions.
prevention: Use certified disease-free seed. Plant locally resistant or tolerant varieties, if available. Plant early to reduce the severity of the disease. Adjust spacing between rows and plants to increase air circulation and reduce moisture in the canopy. Monitor the garden for signs of disease and prune infected plants as soon as they are discovered. Do not over-fertilize with nitrogen. Increase internal aeration nhà kính. Duy trì độ ẩm tương đối dưới 85% và nhiệt độ về đêm cao hơn nhiệt độ bên ngoài (áp dụng với nhà kính). Sử dụng hệ thống tưới nhỏ giọt và tránh tưới nước trên vòm lá. Sử dụng cọc, dây, hoặc tỉa bớt cây để giữ cho cây luôn đứng thẳng và cho phép không khí lưu thông quanh cây. Loại bỏ và tiêu hủy (đốt) mọi tàn tích của cây sau khi thu hoạch. Vệ sinh khu nhà kính giữa những mùa vụ và duy trì các tiêu chuẩn vệ sinh nghiêm ngặt đối with garden tools and equipment as well as for garden workers.
summary: Light green to pale yellow spots spread on the upper surface of the leaves. Dark green, purple-gray patches appear on the underside of the leaves. Leaves dry and curl.

title: Tomato Stem Rot Disease
symptoms: Infection symptoms usually occur on stems close to or just above the ground, but leaves touching the soil can also be affected. First, brown sunken spots appear on the stem. As the disease progresses, these lesions can peel off the bark of the stem, causing the plant to gradually wilt and wither. Small black spots appear on the affected tissues. Lesions or (secondary) cankers can then spread to higher parts of the stem. Splashing water causes disperse fungal spores to other plants, leading to additional infections and spread of the disease. Affected fruits turn black and begin to shrivel.
organic_control: Preventive measures are the best option to prevent disease or minimize the possibility of fungal infections. Some varieties of the fungus Trichoderma harzianum are an effective measure to control D. lycopersici, contributing to maintaining crop productivity.
chemical_control: Always consider the possibility of a combination of preventive measures and biological treatments, to the extent applicable. Preventive measures are the best way to avoid disease or minimize the possibility of fungal infections. Fungicide treatments can be effective if used promptly. Chlorotalonil-based medicinal products can be useful to prevent new infections.
cause: The above symptoms are caused by a fungus with the scientific name Didymella lycopersici. This fungus can exist in soil and infected plant residue. The fungus easily penetrates the plant through mechanical injuries caused by the cultivation process, such as pruning. The alternative host plants of this fungus are limited to tomato, eggplant and potato (nightshade) plants. Spots on the leaves contain patches of fungus. Fungal spores are spread by wind or water to healthy plants. It is also thought that this disease can be spread by infected seeds. However, cold temperatures (20°C), wet weather, rain showers or the use of overhead sprinkler systems are optimal factors for the fungus to spread. Mature plants are also more susceptible to this disease, and soil conditions are deficient in nitrogen and phosphorus can make the disease worse.
prevention: Use healthy seeds from certified sources. Grow disease-resistant varieties. Leave enough space for plants to grow when growing plants in a greenhouse to ensure canopy ventilation. Avoid letting lower leaves touch the soil by removing or pruning them. Water plants in the morning and avoid overhead sprinklers. Make sure to practice crop rotation with multiple plants for at least three years. Plan for drying. ải đất suốt nhiều tuần. Tránh không gây tổn thương cho cây trong thời gian làm việc trên đồng. Loại bỏ các cây bị nhiễm bệnh ngay khi xuất hiện các đốm. Loại bỏ các cây mọc lạc, các loài cỏ dại và các cây ký chủ thay thế trong và xung quanh vườn. Loại bỏ mọi tàn dư cây trồng sau khi thu hoạch và đốt sạch chúng. Đảm bảo vệ sinh sạch sẽ xào/cọc và nông cụ sau khi làm việc trong vườn. Trong nhà kính, duy trì độ ẩm dưới 90% và nhiệt độ above 15°C. |. Use sections of reed stems (Arundo donax) or eucalyptus stakes (Eucalyptus) to keep tomatoes upright, helping to reduce the possibility of infection.
summary: Dark indented spots are clearly visible on the lower part of the stem. Small black spots appear on the affected tissue. The plant gradually wilts and withers. The fruit turns black and shriveled.

title: White Pine Blister Rust
symptoms: Yellow spots appear on the upper leaf surface in early summer and the underside of the leaf displays yellowish blisters. Later, brownish-yellow fungal threads grow on the underside of the leaves. Unlike other fungi, they do not have the form of a film or layer, but are clearly elevated. At a final stage of infection with White Pine Blister Rust, leaves fall off.
organic_control: Remove infected foliage as soon as possible and cut back affected parts of the plant.
chemical_control: There are no fungicides available to treat the White Pine Blister Rust on currants and gooseberries.
cause: White Pine Blister Rust is not a serious disease for alternate hosts such as currants and gooseberries, but is a very serious disease for Pines. The fungal spores are transported by wind and can be carried over very long distances. Fungi generally prefer a warm, humid climate, so locations with good ventilation are needed to dry quickly after rainfall.
prevention: Leave sufficient space between plants to ensure good ventilation. Don’t plant currants and gooseberries in close proximity to pine trees. Plant less vulnerable varieties.
summary: Yellow spots on the underside of the leaves. Brown elevated fungal threads.

title: Caterpillars eat leaves on tomatoes
symptoms: Bite attacks occur throughout the crop cycle and can affect any part of the plant. Larvae prefer the top buds, tender leaves and flowers. On leaves, larvae form irregular whitish-gray leaf tunnels that may later become necrotic. Larvae can bore tunnels into the stem, affecting plant growth. On fruit, black marks may be seen at the entry or exit points of the larvae. Open holes This serves as an entry point for secondary pathogens, leading to fruit rot.
organic_control: Several leaf-eating insect predators were found: among others, the parasitic wasp Trichogramma Pretiosum and the aphids Nesidiocoris tenuis and Macrolophus pygmaeus. Several fungal species including Metarhizium anisopliae and Beauveria bassiana attack the eggs, larvae and adults of this pest. Neem seed extract or pesticides containing Bacillus thuringiensis or Spinosad is also effective.
chemical_control: If possible, always consider a combination of preventative measures and biological treatments. Pesticides recommended to control leafminer pests may not be feasible due to the cryptic nature of the larvae, the pest's high reproductive capacity, and the development of resistance. To avoid resistance, use several insecticides in rotation such as indoxacarb, abamectin, azadirachtin, fenoxycarbe+lufenuron.
cause: The leafminer Tuta absoluta is a devastating pest of tomatoes because of its high reproductive ability, up to 12 generations per year. Females can lay up to 300 cream-colored eggs on the underside of leaves. Eggs hatch at a temperature of 26-30ºC and humidity of 60-75%. Larvae are light green with a characteristic black stripe on the back of the head. Under favorable conditions (temperature, humidity), they Completes development in about 20 days. Adults are silvery brown, 5-7 mm long and hide among leaves during the day. Leafminers can survive the winter as eggs, larvae or adults on leaves or in the soil.
prevention: Use uninfected transplanted plants. Use sticky or attractant traps to monitor your fields and capture adults en masse. Pick up and destroy damaged plants and plant parts. Control alternative host plants in and around the field. Plow and cover the soil with plastic mulch or expose the soil to the sun. Remove and destroy infected plants after harvest. To prevent disease introduction from previous crops, wait at least 6 weeks before planting the next crop
summary: Uneven gray-white burrows on young leaves. Then become necrotic. Large tunnels and burrows in the stem. Black spots on the fruit are where worms enter or escape. Growth slows down. Adults are silvery brown, 5 -7 mm long.

title: Eggplant fruit borer
symptoms: The first symptom indicating the presence of this pest in the garden is the wilting of the tips of young shoots due to the feeding activities of young larvae. Later, flowers, flower buds and stems are also attacked. Young larvae bore through the ends of the main veins of large leaves and soft buds to penetrate the stem, causing the phenomenon of "heart death". Adult larvae bore into the fruit and leave small entrance holes filled with dried worm droppings. Fruits are hollow, discolored and filled with worm droppings. Plants may wilt and weaken in severe infestations, causing significant yield loss. Fruits produced from diseased trees may become unsuitable for consumption.
organic_control: Many parasitoids feed on the larvae (worms) of this butterfly L. orbonalis, for example Pristomerus testaceus, Cremastus flavoorbitalis, and Shirakia schoenobic species. Pseudoperichaeta, Braconids, and Phanerotoma species can also be protected and used in the garden. Neem seed extract (NSKE) at 5% or natural insecticide spinosad can also be used on fruit infected with worms. Use nets with adhesive substances such as glue to hang about 10 cm above the trellis to prevent butterflies from entering and laying eggs on the tree. If there is no sticky net, use a 40cm net throughout the height of 2m and then drop the net at an angle of 80 -85 degrees compared to the vertical net.
chemical_control: In all cases, the possibility of integrating preventative measures and applicable biological control measures should be considered. Different treatment measures should be applied depending on the stage of infestation and the season. Spray sevimol (0.1%), or malathion (0.1%) at regular intervals to control the infestation process. Synthetic pyrethroids should not be used nor should pesticides be used on time of fruit ripening and harvest.
cause: The destructive damage arises from the larvae of the moth species with the scientific name Leucinodes orbonalis. In spring, the female moth lays single eggs or clusters of creamy white eggs on the underside of leaves, on stems, flower buds or fruit bases. After 3 to 5 days, the eggs hatch into larvae (worms) that bore straight into the fruit. Mature larvae have strong pink bodies and brown heads. At the end of the biting stage to feed, the larvae form. Pupae in tough, gray cocoons are attached to stems, dry buds or fallen leaves. The pupa stage lasts from 6 to 8 days, then the adult butterfly lives for about 2 to 5 days, completing its life cycle lasting from 21 to 43 days, depending on environmental conditions. In a year, they can create up to five successive generations and create cumulative damage to plants This pest hibernates underground and feeds on many plants in the Solanaceae family, including tomatoes and potatoes.
prevention: Choose to plant resistant or highly resistant varieties available in the area. Intercrop susceptible host crops to this pest with other crops such as fennel, omum, coriander and black cumin, if two-season planting is possible. Monitor the garden regularly for symptoms of the pathogen. Infected leaves, buds or fruit should be picked offthe plant and destroyed away fromthe garden. Clean up fallen leaves, buds and fruit onthe garden soil. |. For heavily infested trees, it is necessary to remove and destroy them |. Use nylon net barriers to prevent butterflies from entering or migrating to other gardens.
summary: Bite marks appear on flowers and fruits. Stems and tips of young shoots wither. Holes in and out of burrows inside the fruit are covered by worm feces. Fruits are hollow and filled with worm feces. Larvae (worms) have pink bodies and brown heads.

title: Corn pests
symptoms: Corn borers favor the earing stage of the host plant but will also attack the foliage. The larvae feed on the corn silk and then bore holes into the corn, where they begin to eat all the kernels. They can be seen boring around or along the corn creating a series of broken corn kernel marks and leaving behind a row of brown weevil droppings. They are cannibalistic so there is usually only one worm per cob. Numerous holes appear at the tip of the cob. and on developing leaf blades. Because the caterpillars feed on flower structures and corn kernels, impeding pollination and grain filling, yield can be severely compromised. Damage creates an ideal environment for plants to become infected with other diseases.
organic_control: Red-eyed bees and parasitic Telenomus wasps can help control pest populations to a certain extent by infesting the eggs of the corn borer Helicoverpa zea. Larval parasites also exist. Other beneficial insects, such as green-eyed lacewings, big-eyed stink bugs or stink bugs are predators of eggs and small larvae. Some beneficial nematodes are also active when inoculated into corn tips. Germplasm agents The fungal disease Nomuraea rileyi and polyhedron virus also reduce Helicoverpa zea populations. The use of biological pesticides containing Bacillus Thuringiensis or spinosad will be effective if applied promptly. Mineral oil or neem oil sprayed on the corn silk of each ear can also prevent corn cob borer invasion.
chemical_control: Use an integrated approach between preventative measures along with biological control whenever possible. In the field, chemical control is rarely recommended because the larvae hide inside the cob and cannot come into contact with the drug. Insecticides containing pyrethroids, spinetoram, methomyl, esfenvalates or chlorpyrifos can be used.
cause: Corn borers overwinter as pupae in the soil, at a depth of 5 to 10 cm. Healthy adult moths begin to emerge in early spring and are mainly active in the evening and at night, becoming more active as temperatures increase. They have light brown, sometimes olive forewings. A wavy dark brown streak runs along the wing, a few millimeters from the wing edge. The hind wing is grayish white and has a streak. broad black band, with yellow spots at the edge. Females lay single white circular eggs on new corn silk or foliage. Larvae vary in color (from light green to red or brown), are moderately hairy, and are about 3.7 mm long. They have a brown or yellowish-brown head, and their bodies are covered with small black spots corresponding to small spines.
prevention: Grow resistant or disease-tolerant varieties. Plant early to avoid peak moth populations. Monitor the presence of moths and capture them en masse with light traps or traps containing attractants. Keep pesticide use to a minimum to preserve beneficial insect populations. Simultaneous pruning of moth-attracting plants can also be effective. Control weeds in and around fields. Cultivate the land between seasons to expose pupae to under-seasons. blood, birds and other predators.
summary: The larvae feed on the corn silk (corn silk) and then bore holes into the corn. Holes and a line of weevil droppings may be seen around or along the top of the corn. There is usually no more than one larva per cob. Leaves may also be affected, creating an ideal environment for infection with other diseases.

title: Green caterpillars eat pumpkin leaves
symptoms: The young larvae of the caterpillar destroy the chlorophyll portion of the leaves. It then folds the leaves and spins a silk web to hold the leaves together. This pest also attacks developing flowers and fruits. They damage the fruit's skin and cause the fruit to rot. Leaves whose chlorophyll portion has been destroyed by the worm will gradually wither.
organic_control: Collect and destroy pests on plants. Use biological pesticides such as Bacillus thuringiensis and Beauveria bassiana. Use pesticide products derived from plants such as neem, fish medicine, insecticide chrysanthemum and chili. Create growth and activity conditions for parasitic wasps of the genus Apanteles spp. Spray a mixture of cow urine diluted with water on plants to repel insects.
chemical_control: In all cases, the possibility of integrating preventative measures with possible biological control measures should be considered. Spray with insecticides such as malathion (50 EC @ 500 ml/ha), dimethoate (30 EC @ 500 ml/ha) or methyl demeton (25 EC @ 500 ml/ha). Another option is to use insecticides containing cyantraniliprole.
cause: The damage mentioned above comes from young larvae of leaf-eating armyworms. Adult butterflies of this pest have clear white wings with wide dark patches on the edges. Females have orange hairs on the anus. They lay single eggs or lay eggs in clusters on the underside of leaves. About 3 -4 days later, the eggs hatch into elongated caterpillars with a pair of stripes running along the midline of the back. The caterpillars reach maturity in about 10 days. day.
prevention: Monitor the garden to detect the parts between leaf veins that have been eaten by caterpillars. Look for caterpillar droppings between the leaves. Collect and destroy newly developed caterpillars in the early stages. When you detect curled leaves, remove or crush them to kill the pests inside..
summary: The leaves are entwined with silk threads. When the plant is seriously infested with worms, holes appear on the developing melon.

title: Fall green worms
symptoms: Fall armyworm larvae cause great damage because they can eat all parts of the plant. Initially, the young larvae feed on the leaf tissue on one side of the leaf, not reaching the epidermis of the other leaf side (patch feeding). They can feed on seedlings until the buds and branching nodes are destroyed. As they grow, the larvae leave the leaf patch they have eaten and attack other leaf edges, while leaving behind trails of their excrement. They also can eat off tree roots and attack reproductive parts as well as young fruits. In heavily infected trees, leaf-eating caterpillar larvae can cause severe defoliation.
organic_control: Natural enemies of leaf-eating insects are parasitic wasps such as Cotesia marginiventris, Chelonus texanus and C. remus; the most common parasitic fly is Archytas marmoratus; predators such as beetles, spiny stink bugs, flower bugs (scientifically known as Orius insidiosus), birds and rodents. Biological insecticides that can be used are neem essence, Bacillus Thuringiensis or Baculovirus Spodoptera can also be sprayed with Spinosad or Azadirachtin compounds, placing red sand, rock salt, coal powder or ash dust in the petal ring can prevent and kill larvae (with 100%, 98%, 90 and 80% effectiveness in the above order respectively).
chemical_control: Always consider a combination of preventive measures and biological treatment measures, to the extent applicable. Suggested pesticides for use are esfenvalerate, chloropyriphos, malathion and lamba-cyhalothrin. Farmers should use toxic baits made from these pesticides to kill mature insect larvae.
cause: The butterflies of this leaf-eating caterpillar lay about 100 -300 clusters of eggs stuck together on the underside of the leaves. The eggs are usually covered with scales. When hatched, the larvae are light brown or dark green, with stripes running along the flanks and a yellow line running along the back. When they turn into butterflies, they have clear white hind wings and brown forewings with stripes and spots of different shades. Each butterfly's forewing has a pale white spot on it. right at the tip of the wing, it is easy to recognize. Diet and temperature are factors that determine the length of different developmental stages in their life cycle. Cool, humid spring leads to a hot and humid climate, which is a favorable condition for the development life cycle of this caterpillar.
prevention: Grow varieties with high insect resistance. Regularly observe to detect the appearance of caterpillars and use lights or insect traps (10 per hectare) to catch them in large numbers. Plant early to avoid the cyclical peak of caterpillar population growth. Weed growth should be controlled. Harvest early to avoid too much damage. Plow the soil deeply to expose caterpillars and pupae under temperature conditions. high.
summary: Young larvae feed on leaf tissue from one side of the leaf, not the other side. They can feed on seedlings until the buds and branching nodes are destroyed. When grown, the larvae leave the leaf patch they have eaten and attack other leaf edges. They also attack the reproductive parts of the plant and young fruits.

title: Fruit rot disease in tomatoes
symptoms: Tomato fruit rot virus disease is characterized by the appearance of an abnormal patch at the bottom of the fruit. This patch has many different colors and sizes. In the early stages, it is light green. Later, it turns brown and then gradually black as the fruit ripens. The fruit tissues lose their firmness, become sunken and eventually form a flat bottom of the fruit. Black decay inside also develops in the fruit although there is no or little of it. Symptoms appear externally.
organic_control: Apply calcium-rich substances such as algal limestone, basalt powder, calcined lime, dolomite, gypsum or lime slag to the soil.
chemical_control: If possible, always consider a solution that combines preventative measures with biological treatment measures. Spraying calcium chloride on leaves is an emergency countermeasure, do not spray at too high a dose or too frequently.
cause: Tomato fruit rot virus is a physiological disorder caused by a lack of calcium in fruit tissues, unrelated to any insect or pathogen. Calcium increases the strength and firmness of tissues. Calcium deficiency can occur due to insufficient calcium in the soil or the plant's inability to absorb this nutrient from the soil and transfer it to the fruits. This leads to the destruction of tissue structure with consequent formation of forming characteristic sunken and dark areas on the fruit. Irregular watering or damage to the root system can cause calcium deficiency.
prevention: Adjust soil pH, for example by applying lime. Use mulch to keep the soil moist. Avoid damaging the roots during cultivation and care of plants. Make sure the fertilizer regimen is low in nitrogen and high in calcium. Make sure to water regularly during dry periods. Do not over-water and ensure effective drainage of the garden. Use nitrate nitrogen fertilizers instead of ammonium nitrogen fertilizers.
summary: Brown or gray stains appear on the bottom of green fruit. Black rot inside the fruit.

title: Boron deficiency in rice plants
symptoms: Symptoms vary, depending on the cultivar and its growing conditions, but in general they may appear first in new growth. The first sign is usually discoloration and thickening of young leaves. Yellowing may appear uniform or spread in patches between leaf veins, discoloring gradually away from the main vein. Leaves and stems near the shoot apex become brittle and break easily when bent. Leaves may become puckered (slightly raised at the ends). areas between leaf veins), tips and lateral leaf edges may curl down. In some cases, leaf veins may appear thick and convex, leaf stalks may be shortened, causing leaf density to be thicker at the top layers, root buds die, leaves become thickened, flowers do not form, and at a more severe level, boron deficiency causes tissue death at the growing points of the plant do not grow and may crack if the plant continues to lack Boron.
organic_control: Ensure maintenance of soil quality with satisfactory organic matter content and good water holding capacity by applying composted manure.
chemical_control: Other suggestions:
cause: Symptoms of Boron deficiency are often observed in soils with high pH, because plants do not absorb this chemical element in such soil conditions. Soils with low organic matter content (<1.5%) or sandy soils (which are susceptible to leaching of nutrients) are also susceptible to Bo deficiency. Adding additional Bo may not improve the deficiency in such cases because the plant still cannot absorb Boron. Canopy symptoms Leaves may look similar to other diseases: spider mites, zinc deficiency or mild iron deficiency. On tuberous roots, swelling and cracks can also be symptoms of root-knot nematodes or rapid changes in soil moisture can also lead to the death of shoots and root tips, but the young leaves below the shoot tips do not thicken and do not form yellow patches between the veins.
prevention: Avoid growing in soils that have a high pH and are rich in clay, iron or aluminum oxide. Avoid creating high air humidity and low soil moisture. Do not over-fertilize or lime the soil. Avoid over-watering your crops. Regularly test the soil to gain a thorough understanding of the soil nutrient levels in your field.
summary: The leaves are thick and yellow. The leaves and stems become brittle.

title: Magnesium deficiency in rice plants
symptoms: The first symptoms usually appear on older leaves and then on young leaves. Magnesium-deficient plants often form pale green or yellowing streaks on the leaf flesh of older leaves, often starting to spread from the leaf edges. In cereal plants with a mild magnesium deficiency, their leaves form a long green streak that gradually progresses to yellowing of the leaf flesh, the plant often has yellow leaves due to chlorophyll deficiency. In severe cases, the condition This yellowing condition spreads to the middle of the leaf and small leaf veins are also affected. Pale red or brown spots gradually appear on the leaf blades. Later, the yellowed areas develop into necrotic areas, causing the leaves to become ragged and deformed.
organic_control: Use substances containing magnesium such as algal limestone, dolomite or magnesium limestone. Use fertilizers, organic mulches or compost to balance the nutrient content in the soil. They contain organic matter and many nutrients that can be released into the soil at a slow rate.
chemical_control: Use soil or foliar fertilizers that contain magnesium to supplement plants. Magnesium oxide is a compound that can help nutrients be released at a slow rate and is often used in mixed forms to provide magnesium directly to plants. Magnesium sulfate has a magnesium release period of several weeks, so it is an ideal choice to meet the need for quick magnesium supplementation for plants, for example such as spraying magnesium sulfate (MgSO4) (at a dosage of 10g/liter of water) twice a day, each session 10 days apart.
cause: Magnesium deficiency is a common problem in light-textured soils, sandy or acidic soils, poor in nutrients and poor water retention capacity. In such soils, nutrients can easily be washed away. Soils that are rich in potassium, rich in nitrogen or supplemented with excess of these nutrients can also be the cause, because these substances are antagonistic to magnesium in the soil. Magnesium is involved in the process of transporting sugars in plants and is an important component in the chlorophyll molecule. When the plant does not have enough magnesium, it begins to reduce chlorophyll in old leaves to concentrate on growing new leaves. That's why the light intensity that the plant receives also affects the progression of the above symptoms.
prevention: If necessary, check soil pH and lime content to achieve optimal levels. Plan to drain fields effectively and do not over-water plants. Avoid applying too much fertilizer containing potassium carbonate. Use organic mulch to stabilize soil moisture.
summary: Pale green or yellow spots appear on the leaves -starting from the leaf edges. The main leaf veins remain green. Red or brown spots appear on the leaf blade. Leaf tissue dries and then dies. Leaves become deformed.

title: Bugs damage Sugarcane leaves
symptoms: Webbing is formed all over the underside of the leaf, parallel to the leaf veins. The web density is denser towards the leaf tips. Newly formed webs are white, later turn brown and are finally blown off the leaf surface leaving white patches parallel to the leaf veins. The beetles feed on leaves by breaking the cuticle and absorbing the water inside the leaf. Heavily infected leaves appear barren and then wither completely. The beetles are gray in color due to their activity. webs, leaving patches of peeled skin and pieces of soil trapped in the web below the leaf surface. The beetles appear on the underside of the leaves in small, oval clusters, covered by a thin layer of web and arranged unevenly on both sides of the leaf veins. The webs of beetles that survive on the tree under adverse climatic conditions will contribute to a rapid increase in infestation density.
organic_control: Scolothrips indicus Pr is a natural enemy that can destroy eggs embedded in this beetle's web. Spraying with calcium sulfur, fish oil soap or Kelthane may also be effective.
chemical_control: In all cases, the possibility of combining preventative measures with possible biological control measures should be considered. Spray Parathion or Chlorbensite on plants with a diluted solution.
cause: The above mentioned damages are caused by beetles. They mate immediately after the last molt. Eggs are laid one at a time and are attached to the leaves by the web. About 24 hours after mating, they lay eggs. Females lay eggs within 1 to two days and then stop. Each female lays about 40 -60 eggs. Development time in the pupal stage ranges from 10 -12 days. Bugs go through three pupal stages before reaching the final stage. fully mature. Bug activity declines markedly throughout winter and into early summer.
prevention: Pruning off infected sugarcane leaves in the early stages of infestation. Removing weeds growing in and around sugarcane fields, especially Johnson grass (Baru grass)
summary: White webs appear on the leaf's abdomen. Leaves turn white into white patches due to being eaten by bugs.

title: Apricot Chlorotic Leaf Roll
symptoms: Symptom and evolution of the disease vary greatly according to the crop and varieties in question, the environmental conditions, the age of the orchard and the rootstock on which the grafting was made. Initially, a yellowing and rolling of leaves along the midrib axis is observed, often localized on some isolated twigs. Irregular interveinal chlorosis can also be seen in some cases. The untimely appearance of buds in general and the breaking of leaf buds before flower buds in particular are other clear signs of the disease. Later on, several disorders are patent on branches, such as the growth of weak, slender shoots with small leaves and the proliferation of irregularly distributed dried twigs among healthy ones on branches. Even though the outer bark looks normal, the vascular tissues of branches develops discolorations that are evident when the wood is exposed. Premature defoliation of these branches may occur, and they may later dry and fall.
organic_control: To this day, there is no biological treatment for this disease.
chemical_control: Always consider an integrated approach with preventive measures together with biological treatments if available. There is no treatment against this disease once detected, the best way to avoid it is to be thorough with the choice of grafting material.
cause: The symptoms of this disease are triggered by a type of bacteria called Phytoplasma prunorum. Infected planting material is the main source of introduction into healthy areas over long distances. This can be via young plants, budwoods for grafting and especially vegetatively propagated rootstocks. Another potential means of propagation at the level of the orchard seems to be through some species of leafhoppers such as Neoaliturus haematoceps and/or Neoaliturus fertestratus and less commonly Psammotettix striatus and Austroagallia sinuata. This disease can be particularly devastating since trees can be killed within 12 to 24 months after first appearance of symptoms. The rootstock also determine the severity of symptoms, and this period may be reduced to weeks if the rootstock is peach. It is important in the Mediterranean area.
prevention: Budwoods for grafting should be obtained from healthy trees and preferably not grafted on peach rootstocks. Monitor the orchard for sign of the disease and report to the competent authority in case of detection of the symptoms.
summary: Yellowing, rolling of leaves along the midrib axis, along with irregular interveinal chlorosis can be observed. Untimely appearance of buds and breaking of leaf buds before flower buds. Growth of weak, slender shoots with small leaves and proliferation of irregularly distributed dried twigs. Discoloration of inner wood, even though the bark looks healthy.

title: Bacterial Spot on Stone Fruits
symptoms: Symptoms vary widely depending on the species of tree affected. Initially, small, water-soaked and grayish spots appear on the lower leaf side, most commonly near the tip or along the midrib. Over time, they become angular to irregular and purple, brown or black. Sometimes their centers become necrotic and fall out, leaving ragged "shot-hole". Severely infected leaves become chlorotic or scorched and may drop early, causing defoliation. Small, round, sunken spots form on the fruit, sometimes only on the side exposed to the sun. They are olive-brown to black and frequently surrounded by a water-soaked margin. As the fruit matures, they harden and in some cases, exude a yellowish gum after rainy periods. Opportunistic pathogens may colonize cracked or damaged fruit and cause them to rot. Pustules appear on twigs and later develop into cankers that release bacterial ooze.
organic_control: Sorry, we don't know of any alternative treatment against Xanthomonas arboricola . Please get in touch with us in case you know of something that might help to fight this disease. We are looking forward to hearing from you.
chemical_control: Always consider an integrated approach with preventive measures together with biological treatments, if available. There are no completely successful spray programs for the control of bacterial spot. A combination of preventive measures and chemical treatments are necessary to reduce its incidence. Copper-based sprays alone or together with an antibiotic can be used preventively with moderate efficacy. Dosage must be reduced progressively to avoid damage to leaves.
cause: The symptoms are caused by the bacterium Xanthomonas arboricola, whose common hosts are peach, nectarine, prune, plum, and apricot. It overwinters on buds, cracks of the stem or in leaf scars. In spring, when conditions are favorable, it resumes its growth and colonize the rest of the twigs, exuding bacterial ooze as it does so. The ooze is dispersed by dropping dew, wind or splashing rain and carried to healthy twigs, fruits and leaves. The natural pores on leaves or the lenticels on bark are the entry points for the bacteria. Warm temperatures (21 to 29 °C) with light rains, heavy dews, and windy weather are most conducive for disease development and spread. By contrast, the growth of the bacterium and the infection process is impaired when the weather is hot and dry. The disease leads to yield losses, which can be very high in some particularly susceptible varieties.
prevention: Use healthy seeds or planting material from a certified source. Select varieties with some degree of  resistance to the disease. Avoid low-lying or shaded sites with poor air circulation and soil drainage. Prune trees to allow for a better air circulation and to maintain tree vigor. Do not prune during wet weather. Ensure a good vitality in young plantations. Use ground covers or windbreaks to reduce plant damages by blowing soil particles due to strong winds.
summary:
Small water-soaked grayish areas on the underside of the leaves.
Mature spots are angular and are numerous at the tip ends and along the mid rib of leaves. Severe defoliation often results in reduced fruit size and increased sunburn and fruit cracking.

title: Bacterial spot disease in tomatoes
scientific_name: Xanthomonas spp. & Pseudomonas syringae pv. tomato
symptoms: Bacteria attack the foliage, stems and fruits of tomato plants. The first symptoms are the appearance of small yellowish green lesions on young, deformed and often twisted leaves. On older leaves, the lesions do not spread through the leaf veins, gradually taking on an angular shape. At first, they are dark green, slimy and often surrounded by a yellow halo. They appear more often on the leaf edges or tips. Under favorable conditions, they quickly spread and reach a width of 0.25 -0.5 cm and turn light brown and then reddish brown. Finally, the spots look like large holes due to the dry, brittle center. scales.
organic_control: Treatment of bacterial spot disease is difficult and very expensive. If the disease appears early in the season, consider destroying all plants in the season. Bactericides containing copper create a protective layer on foliage and fruit. There are phages (bacterial parasitic viruses) available that are capable of killing the bacteria that cause this disease. Soak the seeds for about a minute in 1.3% sodium hypochlorite solution, or Soaking seeds for about 25 minutes in hot water (50°C), can reduce the possibility of disease.
chemical_control: Always consider the possibility of combining preventive measures and biological treatment measures, to the extent applicable. Copper-containing biocides can be used to prevent the disease and partly control the development of the disease. When using the drug as soon as the first signs of disease symptoms are detected, then continue to use it several times, each time 10 to 14 days apart, when weather conditions Prolonged heat and humidity. When bacteria develop resistance to copper-containing drugs, use a combination of copper-based biocides and mancozeb.
cause: Bacterial spot disease is caused by many species of bacteria in the genus Xanthomonas. The disease occurs worldwide and is one of the most destructive diseases for tomato plants grown in hot and humid environments. The pathogen can exist in or on seeds, on plant residues and on certain weed species. The bacteria have a very short survival time, about a few days to a few weeks, in the soil. When conditions are favorable, the bacteria Bacteria infect healthy plants through rainwater or irrigation water from overhead spray systems. They enter plant tissues through pores (stomata) and wounds on leaves. The optimal temperature for bacterial growth is between 25 and 30°C. Once the plant is infected, the disease is difficult to control and can lead to total loss of crop yield.
prevention: If possible, plant seeds from certified disease-free sources. Use local, disease-resistant varieties. Monitor the garden regularly, especially during cloudy days. Remove and burn any seedlings or plant parts with leaf spots. Remove weeds in and around the garden. Use mulch to prevent the soil from being contaminated with plant pathogens. Clean tools and equipment. Do not use overhead sprinkler systems and avoid working in the garden when foliage is still wet. Plow deeply to bury plant residues after harvest or remove plant residues and let the soil rest for a few weeks or a full month (soil drying).
summary: Small yellowish green lesions appear on young leaves. Leaves are deformed and twisted. Dark, watery lesions with yellow halos appear on older leaves and fruit.

title: Canker disease of tomatoes
symptoms: Infected seedlings develop weak and stunted plants with small white spots on leaf veins and petioles. Symptoms that appear on mature plants may result from the primary infection spreading to new tissues (according to the plant's functional systems) or from secondary infection. Yellowing of leaves between leaf veins, curling of older leaves, and wilting (sometimes only on one side of the leaf) are characteristic signs of systemic infection. Later, the leaves gradually turn brown and wilt. Usually, the leaf stalks remain green and attached to the stem. Later symptoms of infection are characterized by dark brown lesions on the leaf edges and rotting of the leaf base and brown and dark brown stripes. Later, the stem cracks and long brown ulcers appear on the fruit. As the disease progresses, the entire plant withers.
organic_control: Dip the seeds into 8% acetic acid or 5% hydrochloric acid solutions. You can also apply treatments with water or methyl bromide.
chemical_control: Always consider the possibility of combining preventative measures with biological treatments, to the extent applicable. Under conditions of frequent rain and prolonged wet periods, spraying with biocides may improve the disease situation. This can minimize leaf chlorosis and fruit spotting. If preventive measures are taken, the use of copper-based medicinal products offers little benefit, given that these conditions Local infection poses only a small economic threat.
cause: Bacteria can exist on seeds, in plant residues or in the soil. Transmission occurs through contaminated seeds, pathogens in the soil or during plant care. The bacteria reproduce in leaf veins, blocking the transport of water and nutrients in the plant. As a result, the plant begins to wilt and shrivel. High soil moisture and relative humidity conditions and warm temperatures (24 to 32°C) is suitable for the development of the disease.
prevention: Choose certified pathogen-free seeds or transplants. If possible, choose disease-resistant varieties. Soilless cultures on plastic trays can be used instead of garden growing facilities to produce transplants. If possible, fumigation of seed beds and soil to kill bacteria. Clean garden tools and equipment. Remove Solanum weeds. (Solanaceae). Monitor the garden and cut infected plants close to the ground. Rotate tomatoes with non-Solanaceous plants for at least two to three years after harvest and bury infected plant residues.
summary: Yellow leaves, curled and wilted -starting from the leaf edges. Brown spots with halos appear on the fruit. Stem rots with longitudinal stripes. Cracks appear on the stem.

title: Stem rot disease in corn
symptoms: Bacterial stem rot in corn is characterized by discoloration of leaves, leaf sheaths and stem nodes. The disease then grows rapidly along the stem and spreads to other leaves. As the tissues decompose, foul odors can be detected and the tip of the plant can be easily removed from the rest of the plant. The stem rots completely and, in some cases, the plant top falls over. If we cut the stem lengthwise, we have Discoloration and soft rot can be seen inside the stems, mostly concentrated in the nodes. Because the bacteria do not usually spread from plant to plant, infected plants are often found scattered in the field. However, there have been some reports of plant-to-plant spread through some disease-carrying insects.
organic_control: There are currently no biological treatments available to deal with E. chrysanthemi. Please inform us if you know of any that can effectively deal with this disease.
chemical_control: Always consider the possibility of combining preventative measures with biological treatment measures, to the extent applicable. It is recommended to disinfect irrigation water or fertilize the soil with bleaching powder (33% chlorine at a dose of 10 kg/ha) at the pre-flowering stage. Products containing copper oxychloride can also be applied to effectively deal with the disease. Finally, add potassium chloride (MOP) fertilizer. with a dosage of 80 kg/ha divided into two doses can contribute to minimizing the severity of disease symptoms.
cause: The above symptoms are caused by the bacterium Erwinia chrysanthemi. The bacterium overwinters in plant stem residues on the soil surface, but cannot survive longer than a year under such conditions. There is no evidence that the bacterium is transmitted by seeds. The disease develops favorably at temperatures of 32-35 °C and high relative humidity. Frequent rainfall and overhead watering with sprinklers cause damp conditions water accumulates in the leaf ring area. When that standing water heats up, it can damage plant tissues, creating lesions that allow bacteria to infect the tree trunk. Trees exposed to high temperatures or flooding may develop the first symptoms of the disease around the base of the tree. Although the disease can spread along the tree trunk to the nodes, the bacteria are not usually transmitted from tree to tree unless carried by insects. spread.
prevention: Install effective drainage systems to avoid waterlogging. Plant disease-resistant varieties, if available locally. Regularly monitor fields for disease symptoms. Avoid excess nitrogen fertilization and always apply in multiple batches. Adjust fertilization regimens with high levels of phosphorus and potassium to reduce the likelihood of plant disease. Avoid watering during very hot times of the day as water can accumulate inside the leaf ring. In areas endemic, farmers should incorporate green manure into the soil before planting corn. Incorporate crop residue into the soil after harvest to break the disease cycle.
summary: Discoloration appears in the leaves and stem sheaths (leaf bark), then appears in the stem. A foul odor develops and the top of the plant becomes easily separated from the rest of the plant. The inside of the stem becomes discolored and rots.
